narration.default: A function in C is allowed to CALL ITSELF. That sounds weird — even paradoxical — but it works for problems where the answer is "the smaller version of this same problem, plus one step". Think of Russian nesting dolls: each big doll contains a smaller doll inside, which contains an even smaller doll, all the way down to the tiniest one. When a function calls itself, the technique is called RECURSION. Today you'll write a recursive function to compute FACTORIAL: 5! = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120. The recursive insight: 5! = 5 × 4! and 4! = 4 × 3! and so on, all the way down to 1! = 1.

steps.title: The base case stops the chain

narration.default: Lines 4 to 6: if (n <= 1) { return 1; }. This is the BASE CASE — the simplest version of the problem, answered directly. For factorial, 1! = 1 (and 0! is also 1 by mathematical convention). Without a base case, the function would call itself forever and forever, and your program would crash with a "stack overflow" error. EVERY recursive function MUST have a base case. The base case is the bottom of the chain — the smallest doll that doesn't open.

Line 7: return n * factorial(n - 1); — the RECURSIVE call. To compute factorial(5), C asks "what's factorial(4)?". To compute factorial(4), it asks "what's factorial(3)?". And so on down to factorial(1), which hits the base case and returns 1 directly. Then the answers UNWIND back up: factorial(2) = 2 * 1 = 2, factorial(3) = 3 * 2 = 6, factorial(4) = 4 * 6 = 24, factorial(5) = 5 * 24 = 120. Each call waits for the smaller one to finish, then multiplies and returns.

Click Run. Console: "5! = 120". The whole chain happened in microseconds. Try factorial(7) (= 5040), factorial(10) (= 3628800), factorial(12) (= 479001600). Try factorial(20) — you'll get a weird negative number, because 20! is too big to fit in an int. Numbers grow VERY fast with factorial — a classic gotcha. The recursion itself is correct; the int type just can't hold the result. (For bigger numbers, you'd use a long long type — beyond this chapter.)
